elements.sent-2411-001: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has become essential in modern healthcare, with large language models (LLMs) offering promising advances in clinical decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditional model-based approaches, including those leveraging in-context demonstrations and those with specialized medical fine-tuning, have demonstrated strong performance in medical language processing but struggle with real-time adaptability, multi-step reasoning, and handling complex medical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-003: Agent-based AI systems address these limitations by incorporating reasoning traces, tool selection based on context, knowledge retrieval, and both short- and long-term memory.
elements.sent-2411-004: These additional features enable the medical AI agent to handle complex medical scenarios where decision-making should be built on real-time interaction with the environment.
elements.sent-2411-005: Therefore, unlike conventional model-based approaches that treat medical queries as isolated questions, medical AI agents approach them as complex tasks and behave more like human doctors.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper, we study the choice of the backbone LLM for medical AI agents, which is the foundation for the agent’s overall reasoning and action generation.
elements.sent-2411-007: In particular, we consider the emergent o1 model and examine its impact on agents' reasoning, tool-use adaptability, and real-time information retrieval across diverse clinical scenarios, including high-stakes settings such as intensive care units (ICUs).
elements.sent-2411-008: Our findings demonstrate o1’s ability to enhance diagnostic accuracy and consistency, paving the way for smarter, more responsive AI tools that support better patient outcomes and decision-making efficacy in clinical practice.
elements.sent-2411-009: The medical environment requires dynamic artificial intelligence (AI) tools.
elements.sent-2411-010: Foundational large language models (LLMs) like GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 have transformed how the medical field interacts with information technology (IT).
elements.sent-2411-011: Beyond serving as powerful tools for knowledge synthesis \cite{zhong2024evaluation,liu2023holistic , general-purpose LLMs like GPT-4 can achieve excellent accuracy handling domain-specific queries when provided with in-context demonstrations of medical reasoning -- they serve as reasoning engines to support complex decision-making.~\cite{nori2023capabilitiesgpt4medicalchallenge,holmes2023evaluating,li2024echopulse .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, specialized medical models like MedPaLM2 and MedGemini have been trained or fine-tuned on extensive medical datasets and medical textbooks to install medicine-specific reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, both foundational and fine-tuned medical models face limitations that hinder effectiveness in real-world medical applications.~\cite{li2023artificial .}
elements.sent-2411-014: To date, LLMs excel at static tasks (e.g., answering questions based on pre-existing data), but they lack the capability to interact dynamically with the ever-changing medical environment \cite{nori2023capabilitiesgpt4medicalchallenge, liévin2023largelanguagemodelsreason .}
elements.sent-2411-015: These limitations show up in two important limitations: (1) rapid updates in medical knowledge and (2) rapid changes in individual patient conditions.
elements.sent-2411-016: First, the corpus of medical knowledge is constantly changing.
elements.sent-2411-017: Paradigm-shifting studies have been presented at conferences that change the standard of care overnight, despite that guidelines may lag years behind in updates.
elements.sent-2411-018: Indeed, the pace of medical knowledge doubling has shortened from 50 years to just 73 days \cite{densen2011challenges .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Second, critically ill patients have conditions that require rapid assimilation of multi-modal data for assessment and decisions that must occur within minutes to provide life-saving care.
elements.sent-2411-020: Multi-modal data extraction from vital sign flowsheets, laboratory values, medication administration records, and imaging are essential bedside capabilities \cite{10.1093/jamia/ocac064 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Unlike foundational models, LLM agents are characterized by integrated capabilities to interact with a dynamic environment, conduct knowledge-assisted reasoning, and take appropriate actions based on the user’s queries ~\cite{shi2024ehragent, abbasian2024conversationalhealthagentspersonalized, tu2024conversationaldiagnosticai, yang2024psychogat, dai2023ad, xiao2024comprehensivesurveylargelanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-022: LLM agents are equipped with a knowledge base for the retrieval of task-related information and a toolbox of local functions or third-party APIs s~\cite{parisi2022talmtoolaugmentedlanguage, schick2023toolformer .}
elements.sent-2411-023: These agents use one or more LLMs as the backbone for reasoning and subsequent action (e.g., decision for knowledge retrieval or selection of the most appropriate tool).
elements.sent-2411-024: The extendable knowledge base and the adaptive toolbox, integrated with the LLM-based reasoning engine, enable LLM agents to handle complex tasks in diverse application domains, including the medical scenarios discussed herein.
elements.sent-2411-025: Recent evaluations highlight that o1 outperforms its predecessors on a variety of medical datasets, showing particular strength in tasks that require multi-step reasoning, such as those found in the LancetQA and NEJMQA datasets\cite{xie2024preliminarystudyo1medicine .}
elements.sent-2411-026: These findings suggest that o1 could play a major role in refining the performance of AI agents in medicine, where reasoning and the ability to interact with the external environment and tool use are essential.
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, o1’s capability to integrate retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) techniques enables it to access and use up-to-date information, making it more adaptable and relevant in clinical settings where current medical knowledge is essential.
elements.sent-2411-028: We explore the practical benefits of integrating o1 into medical AI agents as their backbone by considering the following three agents: Chain of Diagnosis (CoD) \cite{chen2024chain is a medical diagnostics framework designed with a particular focus on its interpretability.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The pipeline of CoD mirrors the cognitive workflows of healthcare professionals, including symptom abstraction, disease retrieval, and diagnostic reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-030: MedAgents \cite{tang2023medagents is a framework that facilitates collaborative medical decision-making through the integration of multiple domain experts via ``role-play''.}
elements.sent-2411-031: MedAgents is characterized by a structured workflow that selects a group of different types of medical specialists, gathers insights from these specialists, aggregates their inputs, and then refines the medical decisions iteratively.
elements.sent-2411-032: AgentClinic serves as a sophisticated framework of agents that simulates real-world clinical environments to evaluate AI-driven language models.
elements.sent-2411-033: It features a different multi-agent pipeline where a patient agent interacts with a doctor agent and a third measurement agent provides clinical test results.
elements.sent-2411-034: We also replaced GPT-4 with o1 for each module of the three agents to further investigate the potential enhancements by o1 in agent diagnostic accuracy, reasoning consistency, and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-035: The key findings from our experiments reveal several improvements with the integration of o1:.
elements.sent-2411-036: By leveraging advanced techniques such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), o1 effectively handles complex diagnostic queries and simulates clinical workflows, particularly excelling in multi-step reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-037: This suggests a potential for greater reliability in diagnostic tasks across diverse scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, its increased computational demands make it less efficient for these straightforward operations, where simpler models like GPT-4 may suffice.
elements.sent-2411-039: Together, these findings convey the advantages of o1 in elevating diagnostic performance and reliability across varied medical scenarios, pointing to its value in high-stakes clinical applications.
elements.sent-2411-040: While also revealing its limitations that can be improved in future work.
elements.sent-2411-041: By building on the preliminary findings that highlight o1’s superior performance, this study seeks to demonstrate how these improvements translate into real-world healthcare settings.
elements.sent-2411-042: Ultimately, by harnessing the advanced reasoning and adaptability of o1, we can move closer to developing AI agents that meet the demanding needs of modern medicine.
elements.sent-2411-043: In recent years, significant progress has been made in the application of LLMs within the medical field \cite{li2023artificial,liu2023evaluating,liu2023holistic .}
elements.sent-2411-044: These advancements have seen LLMs being applied in various medical domains such as computer-aided diagnosis, genotyping and phenotyping, drug discovery, medical evidence summarization, and treatment planning in radiation oncology \cite{zhang2024generalist,liu2023radiology,dai2023ad,wu2023exploring,liu2023pharmacygpt,ma2024iterative,lyu2024gp .}
elements.sent-2411-045: However, despite their potential, LLMs still face challenges when confronted with clinical tasks that require complex reasoning and specialized medical domain knowledge \cite{zhong2024evaluation,zhao2023brain , both of which are essential for enhancing LLMs' effectiveness across clinical scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Currently, two major research directions exist in this area: tool-augmented methods \cite{lewis2020retrieval and instruction-tuning methods \cite{alpaca .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the tool-augmented approach, LLMs are equipped with external tools to gather additional medical information for better reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-048: For example, GeneGPT \cite{jin2024genegpt integrates Web APIs from the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) to meet biomedical information needs.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Similarly, Almanac \cite{zakka2024almanac integrates a RAG framework with GPT-4 designed for accessing medical guidelines and treatment recommendations.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Another notable method, KARD \cite{kang2024knowledge , enhances domain-specific knowledge by fine-tuning smaller language models with rationales from LLMs and augmenting them with external knowledge from non-parametric memory sources.}
elements.sent-2411-051: On the other hand, instruction-tuning research leverages external clinical knowledge bases and synthetic datasets to create specialized instruction datasets.
elements.sent-2411-052: These datasets are used to fine-tune LLMs for specific medical applications.
elements.sent-2411-053: Some models are trained on a vast array of medical and biomedical literature to gain broad medical insights \cite{li2023artificial,li2024llava , while others focus on specific fields such as radiology report generation for task-oriented design \cite{liu2023radiology,lyu2024gp .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In contrast to these methods, our work aims to leverage the latent medical knowledge within LLMs and improve clinical reasoning in a training-free setting.
elements.sent-2411-055: In addition to the existing literature, recent studies like the one by Xie et al. \cite{xie2024preliminarystudyo1medicine have provided a broad evaluation of advanced LLMs, such as o1, specifically examining their diagnostic and reasoning capabilities across diverse medical scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-056: While these studies highlight the potential of LLMs in medicine by focusing on understanding, reasoning, and handling clinical data, our study emphasizes the performance of LLMs within the context of agent systems.
elements.sent-2411-057: Here, each agent must not only apply medical knowledge but also engage dynamically in knowledge retrieval and reasoning-based tool usage.
elements.sent-2411-058: Thus, while standalone LLMs demonstrate impressive capabilities, integrating them into agent-based frameworks requires models adept at collaborative decision-making.
elements.subsection-2411-002: LLM Agents and Their Applications in the Medical Domain
elements.sent-2411-059: Recent advancements in LLM agents have enabled these systems to act autonomously in complex environments by leveraging advanced reasoning capabilities and memory-based reflection.
elements.sent-2411-060: These agents can dynamically respond to new information, process complex inputs, and make decisions based on their understanding of context \cite{yao2023react, shinn2023reflexion .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Moreover, by utilizing memory, they can maintain continuity across multi-turn interactions, adapting their responses in line with previous exchanges—much like a human professional would in an ongoing consultation~\cite{zhang2024surveymemorymechanismlarge .}
elements.sent-2411-062: A critical feature of effective LLM agents is their ability to reason through complex tasks using multi-step processes.
elements.sent-2411-063: Approaches like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting have been developed to enhance the reasoning depth of LLMs, allowing them to break down problems into sequential steps that mirror human logical reasoning \cite{wei2022chain .}
elements.sent-2411-064: This method has proven especially valuable in the medical field, where diagnostic reasoning requires careful consideration of patient symptoms, history, and test results.
elements.sent-2411-065: By generating intermediate steps, CoT prompting enables LLM agents to better navigate the complexities inherent in medical decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the medical domain, specialized LLM agents like Med-PaLM \cite{singhal2023large and BioGPT \cite{luo2022biogpt have emerged to address the unique demands of healthcare settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: These agents are equipped with capabilities such as advanced knowledge retrieval, adherence to clinical guidelines, and robust multi-step reasoning to support diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Training on large-scale medical datasets further equips these models to interpret clinical notes, answer complex medical queries, and synthesize patient data effectively \cite{chang2023learning .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) plays a critical role here, aligning the model’s outputs with human expertise and judgment.
elements.sent-2411-070: The o1 model, for instance, leverages both CoT and RLHF, allowing it to produce reliable reasoning outcomes—a quality essential for high-stakes clinical applications \cite{zhong2024evaluationopenaio1opportunities .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Beyond individual LLM agents, recent research has explored multi-agent systems within the medical domain, where multiple LLM agents work together to provide more comprehensive clinical insights.
elements.sent-2411-072: These systems are inspired by the collaborative nature of healthcare, where doctors, specialists, and other healthcare providers work in concert to address complex cases.
elements.sent-2411-073: Multi-agent frameworks aim to replicate this teamwork by assigning specific roles to LLM agents, such as patient, doctor, and other necessary roles, which enables them to simulate consultations and cooperative diagnostics \cite{kleinpell2021, ervin2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: By drawing on role-playing techniques from frameworks like Camel, these agents engage in interactive discussions and debates, refining their diagnoses through multi-turn feedback \cite{chen2023universal .}
elements.sent-2411-075: This collaborative approach holds promise for emulating the multidisciplinary decision-making processes crucial in healthcare, ultimately advancing the quality and depth of AI-assisted patient care.
elements.sent-2411-076: Given these advancements, our study leverages agent frameworks with robust reasoning to address the complexities of medical applications.
elements.sent-2411-077: These frameworks, designed to support both independent and cooperative decision-making, reflect real-world healthcare dynamics, where diagnoses and treatment plans often involve multidisciplinary input.
elements.sent-2411-078: By incorporating reasoning, memory-based reflection, collaborative feedback, etc., our chosen frameworks aim to enhance diagnostic accuracy and align closely with the collaborative nature of clinical practice.
elements.sent-2411-079: Overview: The Chain of Diagnosis (CoD) \cite{chen2024chain framework is a medical AI agent designed for automated diagnosis.}
elements.sent-2411-080: It is characterized by a pipeline that simulates the cognitive workflows of healthcare professionals.
elements.sent-2411-081: When a patient is seen in a healthcare setting, the healthcare professional goes through a systematic process summarized by the SOAP format (subjective, objective, assessment, plan) \cite{podder2023soap , which includes collecting subjective symptoms (e.g., sore throat) with objective symptoms (e.g., temperature, congestion) and compares these DiagnosisGPT applies CoD to standardize this assessment process and compares these implicit and explicit symptoms against a disease database to develop a diagnostic reasoning process, a differential diagnosis with confidence distributions, and a final diagnosis.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Similarly, CoD enables a stepwise diagnostic approach with knowledge retrieval and inference based on confidence distribution over the candidate diseases.
elements.sent-2411-083: The framework thus ensures that the diagnostic inference of CoD is grounded in a provided knowledge base, highlighting its potential flexibility in handling emerging medical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-084: Agent Design: From the high level, CoD takes a structured Symptom Abstraction synthesized from the patient’s explicit symptoms as its inputs, and outputs a diagnostic inference with an optional confidence score.
elements.sent-2411-085: The workflow of CoD contains three stages:.
elements.sent-2411-086: These candidate diseases are ranked based on their similarity with the Symptom Abstraction in the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-087: This step significantly narrows down the diagnostic scope, focusing computational resources on the most likely conditions.
elements.sent-2411-088: Specifically, a prompt including the input symptom, the candidate diseases, and the description of each candidate disease is used to query the backbone LLM for a confidence score estimated for each candidate disease.
elements.sent-2411-089: The descriptions for the candidate diseases are stored in an internal knowledge base and can be easily retrieved based on the disease name.
elements.sent-2411-090: In the original CoD paper, the backbone LLM in this stage is fine-tuned on a specially created dataset to learn the mapping from the input symptoms and the candidate diseases to the confidence scores.
elements.sent-2411-091: In our experiments, when evaluating CoD with foundation LLMs (without the special fine-tuning of CoD), we skip this step by directly inferring the most likely disease without generating confidence scores.
elements.sent-2411-092: This distribution not only guides decision-making but also provides an interpretation of the inference results.
elements.sent-2411-093: Again, we simplify the pipeline of CoD in our experiments by directly producing the inference results on the candidate diseases and the input symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-094: Key Features and Potential Gain from o1 Integration: The key features of the CoD framework are its integration of confidence distribution and knowledge retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-095: These two mechanisms critically enhance the internal reasoning of the agent and the accuracy of the diagnostic process, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-096: The confidence distribution assigns probabilities to potential diagnoses, providing clinicians with clear insights into the model’s confidence level for each diagnostic option.
elements.sent-2411-097: Knowledge retrieval allows the model to access a comprehensive database of medical information to support each diagnostic stage.
elements.sent-2411-098: The integration of LLMs with strong reasoning capabilities, such as the o1 model, may further enhance the CoD's decision-making for knowledge retrieval and diagnostic reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-099: In practice, the o1 model can potentially analyze more complex medical scenarios by considering additional factors such as patient history, co-morbidities, and even potential treatment complications.
elements.sent-2411-100: This expanded analytical scope, enabled by the incorporation of o1, allows CoD to make nuanced adjustments to the confidence distribution, dynamically weighting factors based on their clinical relevance and urgency.

elements.sent-2411-101: Agent Settings In this experiment, we adjust the diagnostic framework of CoD to make it more suitable for foundation LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-102: In Step 2, we deviate from the original method of CoD that utilizes embedding similarity to select the top-k relevant diseases.
elements.sent-2411-103: Instead, we retrieve all diseases in the candidate pool provided in the dataset and the relevant descriptions and clinical summaries for each disease.
elements.sent-2411-104: These descriptions are then inputted into the LLM, which performs a ranking operation across all candidate diseases.
elements.sent-2411-105: By evaluating each disease's relevance to the extracted symptoms, the model ranks the candidates and identifies the top-ranked disease.
elements.sent-2411-106: This ranked selection substitutes for the confidence level estimation traditionally used, providing a more interpretable and model-driven method for candidate prioritization.
elements.sent-2411-107: Datasets and Metrics The datasets in use here are Dxbench, Dxy, and Muzhi.
elements.sent-2411-108: Muzhi and Dxy are built based on real doctor-patient consultation.
elements.sent-2411-109: DxBench is built based on the MedDialog dataset, which contains real doctor-patient dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-110: It focuses on assessing models’ ability to predict diseases based on symptoms, allowing for both multiple-choice answers and targeted symptom inquiries.
elements.sent-2411-111: For our evaluation of the Dxy dataset, we conduct tests on a subset of 120 cases.
elements.sent-2411-112: The results are divided into three folds.
elements.sent-2411-113: For each group, we calculate the mean diagnostic accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-114: The overall performance metric is derived by averaging these three group means and calculating the standard deviation across them, providing a robust measure of model performance variability across different subsets.
elements.sent-2411-115: It challenges models to accurately diagnose based on symptoms, enabling both multiple-choice responses and symptom inquiries to improve prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-116: Again we sample 120 sample cases and compute standard deviations like we did in Dxy Dataset.
elements.sent-2411-117: Muzhi primarily employs multiple-choice questions to guide models in selecting the most probable diagnosis or in requesting additional symptom information to improve diagnostic accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-118: For our evaluation of the Muzhi Dataset, we conduct the same as previously.
elements.sent-2411-119: Model Choices We test three models: GPT-4, and o1.
elements.sent-2411-120: In step 2, the model is replaced by the OpenAI backbone models, including both GPT-4 and o1-preview, using input data consisting of patient symptoms and a list of candidate diseases.
elements.sent-2411-121: The models process this data in a multiple-choice question format to select the most likely diagnosis.

elements.sent-2411-129: The experimental results indicate that, while o1 consistently outperforms GPT-4 in terms of diagnostic accuracy across all datasets, this improvement comes at the cost of increased runtime.
elements.sent-2411-130: Specifically, o1’s advanced reasoning and multi-step analytical capabilities require substantially more computational resources, resulting in longer processing times.
elements.sent-2411-131: For instance, in the Dxy dataset, o1’s runtime per entry is notably higher than GPT-4’s, which may impact its practicality in real-time clinical scenarios where rapid decision-making is essential.
elements.sent-2411-132: This increased runtime is a trade-off inherent in o1’s complex reasoning framework.
elements.sent-2411-133: While o1’s enhanced accuracy makes it highly suitable for applications that prioritize diagnostic precision and depth of reasoning, the additional computational overhead may restrict its feasibility in time-sensitive clinical environments.
elements.sent-2411-134: O1 is more stable than GPT-4.
elements.sent-2411-135: The o1 backbone also exhibited a smaller standard deviation (std), indicating that the model generally performs more consistently on the Dxy and DxyBench datasets.
elements.sent-2411-136: In the medical field, stable and consistent diagnostic performance is highly valuable, as it brings the model’s behavior closer to that of human doctors by providing more reliable diagnoses across varied cases.
elements.sent-2411-137: Overview: MedAgents is a medical collaborative agent based on role-play.
elements.sent-2411-138: It simulates the real clinical environment where a group of experts with diverse expertise discuss a patient's description of symptoms and reach a consensus on diagnosis and treatments.
elements.sent-2411-139: The pipeline of MedAgents is composed of five major stages: gathering a group of experts, symptom analysis by each expert, summarization of individual expert's analysis, consultation for mutual agreements, and final decision-making \cite{tang2023medagents .}
elements.sent-2411-140: The simulation of expert discussions with zero-shot reasoning of MedAgents enables it to outperform simple single-agent design on medical datasets and effectively harness latent medical knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-141: Agent Design: From a high level, MedAgents takes clinical questions and patient data as structured inputs, synthesizing insights from various domain experts.
elements.sent-2411-142: The output is a consensus diagnostic inference after possibly multiple rounds of ``discussion'' between the experts.
elements.sent-2411-143: The MedAgents workflow consists of five stages:.
elements.sent-2411-144: This is achieved by querying the backbone LLM with the input description of the patient's symptoms and an instruction to create five experts based on the description.
elements.sent-2411-145: Note that the experts selected by the backbone LLM can be arbitrary without any pool of candidates.
elements.sent-2411-146: Specifically, for each expert, the backbone LLM is prompted with the role of the expert and a description such as the specialized knowledge area of this expert.
elements.sent-2411-147: The backbone LLM is prompted with the analysis generated by the five selected experts and an instruction to aggregate these analyses.
elements.sent-2411-148: The experts are engaged in a ``discussion'' around the summary report.
elements.sent-2411-149: If a consensus (via voting) can not reached, the minority expert will be asked to refine the summary report based on his/her expertise.
elements.sent-2411-150: The refined summary report will be voted on by all the experts again.
elements.sent-2411-151: This voting-refinement procedure is iterated until a consensus or the maximum allowed iteration is reached (whichever comes first).
elements.sent-2411-152: Both the finalized clinical decision (a symbolic option choice for multi-choice questions) and the summary report supporting the decision will be provided to the user.
elements.sent-2411-153: Key Features and Potential Gain from o1 Integration: MedAgents is a role-play-based multi-agent system mirroring real-world clinical workflows.
elements.sent-2411-154: It leverages a symptom-based agent-selection mechanism to identify relevant, field-specific agents and facilitate an in-depth collaborative consultation process.
elements.sent-2411-155: The integration of the o1 model is anticipated to enhance this collaborative diagnostic process by contributing advanced reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-156: With o1’s capacity to incorporate complex factors, such as patient history and co-morbidities, MedAgents aims to achieve increased diagnostic precision and adaptability, yielding more accurate disease diagnosis.

elements.sent-2411-159: Datasets and Metrics MedQA and MedMCQA are high-caliber datasets curated to rigorously benchmark the diagnostic reasoning and specialized medical knowledge processing abilities of large language models (LLMs) within clinical and academic healthcare contexts.

elements.sent-2411-161: To optimize efficiency and computational feasibility, we randomly selected 120 questions from this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-162: This selection ensures a broad representation of medical topics while minimizing processing requirements.

elements.sent-2411-165: Model Choices In this study, we compare GPT-4 and the o1 model (specifically, o1-preview) as the core engines powering MedAgents language agents.
elements.sent-2411-166: GPT-4 is used as the baseline due to its established strengths in language comprehension and reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-167: The o1 model, recognized for its advanced multi-step reasoning and adaptability, is explored as a possible upgrade aimed at enhancing diagnostic accuracy and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-168: By substituting GPT-4 with o1, we assess how effectively the o1 model can boost performance in complex, high-stakes medical scenarios, with a particular emphasis on deepened reasoning and reduced diagnostic variability.

elements.sent-2411-176: Overview: AgentClinic \cite{schmidgall2024agentclinicmultimodalagentbenchmark is a multi-agent system simulating real clinical environments.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Different from MEDAgents which uses a role-play-based approach, AgentClinic involves multiple individual agents interacting directly with each other.
elements.sent-2411-178: The AgentClinic system includes four agents: a patient agent, a doctor agent, a measurement agent, and a moderator agent.
elements.sent-2411-179: The patient agent provides symptoms like a real human patient, while the doctor agent asks questions and consults the measurement agent for test results to refine its diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-180: The moderator agent then validates the doctor’s conclusion, simulating the collaborative, multi-step process typical of real healthcare scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-181: Such a simulation of the real-world clinical environments aims to evaluate how well AI-driven agents manage patient interactions, respond to evolving cases, and make decisions that mirror the flow of actual clinical practice.
elements.sent-2411-182: In a simulated scenario conducted purely through text-based interactions, the patient agent presents a set of symptoms without access to the final diagnosis, requiring the doctor agent to actively engage by asking diagnostic questions, conducting virtual tests, and working toward an accurate diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-183: Agent Design: AgentClinic is a multi-agent diagnostic system designed to emulate real-life clinical workflows through layered information processing.
elements.sent-2411-184: Following a modular design, it comprises four core agents—the patient, doctor, measurement, and moderator agents—each assigned specific roles to build a comprehensive diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-185: The patient agent initiates the process by providing symptom data.
elements.sent-2411-186: The doctor agent interprets and investigates through questions and virtual tests.
elements.sent-2411-187: For objective data, the doctor agent requests test results from the measurement agent.
elements.sent-2411-188: Finally, the moderator agent verifies the diagnosis, ensuring that each step, from data collection to diagnosis validation, mirrors the structured flow of real-world clinical consultations.
elements.sent-2411-189: This is a purely text-based conversational process (See \ref{app:agentclinic ).}
elements.sent-2411-190: The patient agent doesn’t know the end diagnosis, meaning the doctor agent has to dig deep, asking follow-up questions to uncover critical information.
elements.sent-2411-191: This realistic setup requires the doctor agent to fully engage, just like a physician would, creating a lifelike environment\cite{ely2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-192: This prompt directs the agent to analyze patient data step-by-step, synthesizing symptoms, test results, and medical history to produce a ranked list of possible diagnoses.
elements.sent-2411-193: The input consists of patient data gathered from the conversation with the patient agent, including responses and test results from the measurement agent, while the output is a prioritized list of diagnoses refined iteratively as new data is considered.
elements.sent-2411-194: The conversation concludes either when a diagnosis is reached or when the maximum iterations are completed, at which point the doctor agent provides the most probable diagnosis based on prior interactions.
elements.sent-2411-195: The addition of the measurement agent is critical to replicating the multi-dimensional approach required in actual diagnostics.
elements.sent-2411-196: This step is essential for balancing subjective patient-reported symptoms with hard clinical evidence, giving the doctor agent a more accurate basis for its conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-197: It compares the diagnosis against the known outcome, ensuring accuracy even if the doctor’s phrasing is slightly off.
elements.sent-2411-198: This layer of oversight completes the workflow by confirming that the AI’s reasoning aligns with clinical standards and expectations.
elements.sent-2411-199: If the workflow fails the examination, the system will output "diagnosis is incorrect" to avoid misinforming clinicians and ensure any discrepancies are clearly flagged for further review.
elements.sent-2411-200: Key Features and Potential of o1 Model Integration: AgentClinic’s defining qualities include its lifelike patient interaction, structured decision-making flow, and ability to assess AI performance in biased scenarios through reasoning during multi-hop QA.
elements.sent-2411-201: The integration of the o1 model could take this setup even further.
elements.sent-2411-202: By enhancing the doctor agent’s ability to reason through complex cases and make more targeted test selections, o1 could help the AI reach more accurate conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-203: This advanced layer of reasoning may enable the doctor agent to surpass human-level performance in diagnostic accuracy.

Once a diagnosis is reached, the moderator agent verifies it against standard care practices, ensuring clinical accuracy and compliance.} \label{agentclinic_pipe}
elements.sent-2411-204: Agent Settings: In our study, we evaluate and compare the performance of two different LLM backbones, GPT-4 and o1-preview, as the core models for language agents within the AgentClinic framework.
elements.sent-2411-205: This setup involves four key agents—doctor, patient, measurement, and moderator—each responsible for specific tasks in the diagnostic process.
elements.sent-2411-206: We perform two sets of experiments: one where all agents are powered by GPT-4 and another with o1-preview as the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-207: By consistently replacing the backbone model across all agents, we aim to observe how each model impacts overall diagnostic accuracy, reasoning consistency, and adaptability within simulated medical decision-making tasks.
elements.sent-2411-208: Datasets and Metrics: We utilize two primary datasets to benchmark the diagnostic reasoning and specialized knowledge capabilities of each model in clinical contexts: MedQA and the NEJM case challenge dataset.
elements.sent-2411-209: Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE), the MedQA dataset contains 215 multiple-choice questions (MCQs) with four answer options.
elements.sent-2411-210: For efficiency and computational feasibility, we randomly sampled 120 entries from this dataset, ensuring a broad representation of medical topics while minimizing processing requirements.
elements.sent-2411-211: Given that o1-preview does not support image processing, we conducted all experiments without the images to ensure comparability between models.
elements.sent-2411-212: Although this limitation may impact overall accuracy, the NEJM dataset still provides valuable insights into each model’s textual reasoning and diagnostic capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-213: Model Choices: In this study, we compare GPT-4 and o1 (more specifically o1-preview) as the backbone models for AgentClinic’s language agents.
elements.sent-2411-214: GPT-4 serves as the baseline model, given its established capabilities in language understanding and reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-215: The o1 model, known for its enhanced multi-step reasoning and adaptability, is tested as a potential upgrade to improve diagnostic accuracy and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-216: By replacing GPT-4 with o1, we investigate the extent to which the o1 model can enhance performance in complex, high-stakes medical scenarios, particularly focusing on improved reasoning depth and reduced diagnostic variability.

'o1-doctor' denotes that only the doctor agent is powered by o1, with the remaining agents using GPT-4, while 'o1-patient' specifies that only the patient agent utilizes o1 as the backbone LLM, with the others using GPT-4.} \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth} {l X X X X} \multicolumn{2}{c}{MedQA} \multicolumn{2}{c}{NEJM} \cmidrule(lr){2-3} \cmidrule(lr){4-5} Backbone Accuracy (\%) Runtime (s) Accuracy (\%) Runtime (s) GPT-4 59.17 ± 3.12 89.93 25.00 ± 2.04 89.91 o1-doctor 77.50 ± 5.00 112.57 38.33 ± 5.77 138.06 o1-patient 58.33 ± 8.78 207.57 23.33 ± 1.44 202.96 o1-all 75.83 ± 1.18 205.76 35.83 ± 6.24 197.94 \end{tabularx} \label{tab3:agentclinic}
elements.sent-2411-217: Selective replacement of the doctor agent yields significant improvements in diagnostic accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-218: The experimental results show that o1-based backbones, particularly o1-all and o1-doctor, lead to substantial improvements in diagnostic accuracy for AgentClinic compared to GPT-4.
elements.sent-2411-219: On the MedQA dataset, o1-all achieved an accuracy of 75.83\% (±1.18), a considerable increase from GPT-4's 59.17\% (±3.12).
elements.sent-2411-220: For NEJM, o1-all reached 35.83\% (±6.24) versus GPT-4’s 25.00\% (±2.04), demonstrating enhanced capabilities in handling both structured and complex medical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-221: Notably, o1-doctor achieved the highest accuracy on both datasets, with 77.50\% on MedQA and 38.33\% on NEJM, suggesting that upgrading only the doctor agent can capture the diagnostic advantages of o1’s advanced reasoning without additional computational complexity in other agents.
elements.sent-2411-222: The observed higher variance in o1's performance compared to GPT-4 can be attributed to the characteristics of our sampling and testing methodology.
elements.sent-2411-223: Specifically, the variance naturally fluctuates in proportion to the overall accuracy of the testing set, reflecting the inherent variability in the sampled cases.
elements.sent-2411-224: Strategically replacing only the doctor agent provides a balance between accuracy and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-225: While o1-all demonstrated higher accuracy, it also required significantly more computational resources, with runtime per entry more than twice that of GPT-4 for both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-226: In contrast, o1-doctor showed a smaller runtime increase (112.57s for MedQA and 138.06s for NEJM), offering an efficient middle ground with high diagnostic performance but a more manageable computational demand.
elements.sent-2411-227: This improved balance is likely due to the doctor agent’s central role in diagnostic reasoning, making it the most effective place to incorporate o1’s reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-228: By contrast, o1-patient—where only the patient agent uses o1—showed minimal accuracy improvements and similar runtimes to o1-all, indicating that enhancing only the patient agent does not substantially affect diagnostic outcomes due to its more passive, information-providing role.
elements.sent-2411-229: This study investigated the integration of the o1 model into various LLM-based agents within the medical domain, replacing traditional backbone models like GPT-4.
elements.sent-2411-230: Our experiments demonstrated that the o1 model significantly enhances performance across multiple medical reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-231: In the CoD Agent framework, o1-preview not only matched but often exceeded GPT-4 in accuracy, showing more consistent diagnostic results with lower standard deviations.
elements.sent-2411-232: In the MedAgent framework, o1-preview performs better on the MedQA and MedMCQA datasets than GPT-4 and demonstrates greater stability.
elements.sent-2411-233: Similarly, in the AgentClinic simulations, o1-preview achieved higher mean accuracies on both the MedQA and NEJM datasets compared to GPT-4, indicating superior diagnostic capabilities even in complex, multimodal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-234: The future of healthcare envisions a sophisticated, multi-agent LLM framework that enables seamless collaboration across departments and specialties.
elements.sent-2411-235: Leveraging the o1 model's advanced Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques, LLM agents can enhance diagnostic workflows and cross-disciplinary consultations.
elements.sent-2411-236: By embedding CoT reasoning directly into its training process and incorporating reinforcement learning from human feedback, o1-preview demonstrates enhanced multi-step reasoning abilities, effectively managing complex diagnostic queries, simulating clinical workflows, and adapting to dynamic environments like ICUs.
elements.sent-2411-237: Through specialized agents for diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment coordination, future hospital systems can dynamically adapt to real-time patient data, enhance diagnostic precision, and optimize treatment pathways.
elements.sent-2411-238: This collaborative, multi-agent approach promises to transform medical decision-making into a comprehensive, efficient process, aligning interdisciplinary insights to ultimately improve patient outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-239: However, the experiments also highlighted some limitations.
elements.sent-2411-240: The current version of o1-preview lacks support for multimodal data processing, affecting its performance on datasets that include imaging data, such as the NEJM dataset.
elements.sent-2411-241: In the medical field, the ability to process multimodal inputs is essential, as complex clinical tasks often require the integration of various data types, such as radiology images/videos, clinical notes, and speech data, to provide accurate and comprehensive insights.
elements.sent-2411-242: Leveraging the advanced reasoning capabilities of the o1-preview model as the central component of a multimodal agent framework could effectively address the complexities involved in interpreting diverse clinical factors across various scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-243: Additionally, the increased computational runtime with o1-preview indicates a trade-off between accuracy and efficiency, which could be a concern in time-sensitive clinical settings.
elements.sent-2411-244: Future research should focus on addressing these limitations by integrating the o1 model into a multimodal multi-agent framework.
elements.sent-2411-245: This integration would enhance diagnostic capabilities by positioning the o1 model as a task planner that utilizes advanced reasoning to coordinate and assign subtasks across different modalities for various expert models.
elements.sent-2411-246: Furthermore, optimizing computational efficiency without compromising performance is also crucial for practical applications in real-world clinical environments.
elements.sent-2411-247: In conclusion, replacing traditional backbone models with the o1 model in medical AI agents offers significant practical benefits.
elements.sent-2411-248: The o1 model's superior reasoning, adaptability, and consistent performance across diverse clinical tasks position it as a promising tool for advancing healthcare delivery.
elements.sent-2411-249: By continuing to refine models like o1 and integrating them into clinical practice, we move closer to developing AI agents that can meet the complex demands of modern medicine, ultimately enhancing patient care and outcomes.. \bibliographystyle{unsrt .}
